title: AEROSPACE FUNDAMENTALS
description: LEC. 1. LAB. 3. Pr. ENGR 1110 and ( PHYS 1600 or PHYS 1607 ). C or better in PHYS 1600 and ENGR 1110 . Introduction to the fundamental physical concepts required for the successful design of aircraft and spacecraft.

title: AERODYNAMICS I
description: LEC. 3. Pr. MATH 2650 and AERO 2200 . C or better in AERO 2200 and MATH 2650 . Properties of fluids, fluid statics, conservation of mass and momentum, atmospheric properties, two dimensional airfoils, three dimensional wings, drag, and flight performance.

title: AERODYNAMICS II
description: LEC. 3. Pr. ENGR 2010 and MATH 2650 and AERO 2200 . C or better in AERO 2200 , MATH 2650 , and ENGR 2010 . Principles of compressible flow including flows with area changes, friction and heat transfer. Fundamental analysis of aerodynamics and potential flow theory. Correlation of potential flow theory with experimental data.

title: AERODYNAMICS LABORATORY
description: LEC. 1. LAB. 3. Pr. P/C AERO 2200 . C or better in AERO 2200 . Application of fundamental aerodynamic principles to subsonic and supersonic wind tunnel experiments.

title: AEROSPACE SYSTEM DYNAMICS AND CONTROLS
description: LEC. 3. Pr. ENGR 2350 and MATH 2650 and ( MATH 2660 or MATH 2667 ). C or better in ENGR 2350 , MATH 2650 , and MATH 2660 . Modeling of system elements, classical feedback control techniques used in the analysis of linear systems, analysis of systems undergoing various motions connected with flight.

title: FLIGHT DYNAMICS
description: LEC. 3. LAB. 2. Pr. AERO 3110 and ENGR 2350 and MATH 2650 and AERO 3220 . C or better in MATH 2650 and ENGR 2350 . Airplane performance and stability and control including analytical prediction of performance characteristics, experimental determination of static stability parameters, and analytical prediction of dynamic stability characteristics.

title: ORBITAL MECHANICS
description: LEC. 3. Pr. ENGR 2350 and MATH 2650 . C or better in ENGR 2350 and MATH 2650 . Geometry of the solar system and orbital motion, mathematical integrals of motion, detailed analysis of two-body dynamics and introduction to artificial satellite orbits; Hohmann transfer and patched conics for lunar and interplanetary trajectories.

title: AEROSPACE STRUCTURES I
description: LEC. 1. LAB. 3. Pr. ENGR 2070 or ENGR 2077 . C or better in ENGR 2070 . Fundamental concepts employed in the mechanical testing of engineering materials and structures. Load, stress, and strain measurement techniques are utilized to determine material properties and structural response.

title: SPECIAL TOPICS
description: AAB.  SU. Departmental approval. Investigation of various topics in Aerospace Engineering. Course may be repeated for a maximum of 6 credit hours.

title: AERODYNAMICS III
description: LEC. 3. Pr. AERO 3110 . Theoretical background essential to a fundamental understanding of laminar and turbulent boundary layers and their relations to skin friction and heat transfer.

title: AEROSPACE PROPULSION
description: LEC. 3. LAB. 3. Pr. AERO 3120 . Fundamental analysis of airbreathing jet propulsion. Introduction to chemical rocket propulsion.

title: AEROSPACE STRUCTURES II
description: LEC. 3. LAB. 3. Pr. AERO 3610 and ( MATH 2660 or MATH 2667 ). C or better in MATH 2660 . Aircraft and space vehicle structures. An introduction to the finite element method and its application to structural analysis.  The laboratory will utilize state-of-the-art software numerical solution of aerospace structural systems.

title: AEROSPACE STRUCTURAL DYNAMICS
description: LEC. 3. LAB. 3. Pr. AERO 4620 and MATH 2650 . C or better in MATH 2650 . Free, forced and damped vibration of single and multiple degree-of-freedom systems.  The laboratory will utilize state-of-the-art software for the analysis of the vibration and dynamic response of structural systems.

title: AEROSPACE DESIGN I: AIRCRAFT DESIGN
description: LEC. 2. LAB. 3. Pr. AERO 3120 and AERO 3110 and AERO 3610 and P/C AERO 3230 . Introduction to the principles required to design aerospace vehicles.

title: AEROSPACE DESIGN II: AIRCRAFT DESIGN
description: LEC. 2. LAB. 3. Pr. AERO 4710 . This course is continuation of AERO 4710 .

title: AEROSPACE DESIGN I: SPACE MISSION
description: LEC. 2. LAB. 3. Pr. AERO 3120 and AERO 3610 and P/C AERO 3310 . Introduction to the design of space systems including the identification of launch requirements, spacecraft system components, satellite tracking and orbital analysis to achieve a stated scientific objective.

title: AEROSPACE DESIGN II: SPACE MISSION
description: LEC. 2. LAB. 3. Pr. AERO 4730 . A continuation of AERO 4730 , Space Mission Design I.

title: AEROSPACE DESIGN I: LAUNCH VEHICLE
description: LEC. 2. LAB. 3. Pr. AERO 3120 and AERO 3610 and AERO 3310 . Introduction to the principles required to design aerospace vehicles.

title: AEROSPACE DESIGN II: LAUNCH VEHICLE
description: LEC. 2. LAB. 3. Pr. AERO 4750 . This course is continuation of AERO 4750 .

title: SPECIAL TOPICS IN AEROSPACE ENGINEERING
description: AAB. Departmental approval. Investigation of current state-of-the-art technologies in aerospace engineering. Course may be repeated for a maximum of 9 credit hours.

title: HONORS THESIS
description: IND. Pr. Honors College. Departmental approval. Membership in the Honors College and departmental approval required; Directed research and writing of an honors thesis. Course may be repeated for a maximum of 3 credit hours.

title: MISSILE AERODYNAMICS
description: LEC. 3. Pr. AERO 3120 . Coreq. AERO 4140 . Aerodynamics of slender wing-body combinations, interference effects, linear and non-linear effects, applications to missile design and performance.

title: ROTARY WING AERODYNAMICS
description: LEC. 3. Pr. AERO 3110 . Aerodynamics and flight characteristics of rotary-wing aircraft.

title: COMPUTATIONAL FLUID DYNAMICS
description: LEC. 3. Pr. AERO 3110 . An introduction to finite-difference and finite-volume methods for solving partial differential equations of interest in fluid dynamics.

title: FLIGHT SIMULATION
description: LEC. 3. Pr. AERO 3230 . Time domain simulation of nonlinear, six-degree-of-freedom motion of flight vehicles. Development of modular digital simulations including vehicle models for aerodynamics and propulsion, control, guidance subsystems.

title: APPLIED ORBITAL MECHANICS
description: LEC. 3. Pr. AERO 3310 . Introduction to general and special perturbations; N-body and restricted three-body problems; C-W equations, targeting and rendezvous; satellite constellations.

title: AEROACOUSTICS
description: LEC. 3. Pr. AERO 3120 or Departmental approval. Fundamental concepts in acoustics: decibel scales, sound propagation and measurement, plane and spherical waves, room acoustics, transmission and reflection, reverberant fields and noise assessment.  May count either AERO 5410 or AERO 6410 .

title: PERTURBATION METHODS
description: LEC. 3. Pr. MATH 2650 . Analytical solutions of nonlinear problems, ODEs, PDEs, multiple scales, and transcendental equations in engineering, mathematics, and physics using both regular and singular perturbation methods. May count either AERO/ MATH 5460 or AERO/ MATH 6460 .

title: ROCKET PROPULSION
description: LEC. 3. Pr. AERO 4510 . Analysis of the thermodynamics, gas dynamics and design of liquid and solid propellant rocket engines.

title: SPACE PROPULSION
description: LEC. 3. Pr. AERO 4510 . Analysis of space propulsion systems.  Dynamics of electromagnetic systems, ion engines, photon drives, laser propulsion.

title: AEROSPACE APPLICATIONS OF COMPOSITE MATERIALS
description: LEC. 3. LAB. 3. Pr. AERO 3610 . Basic material and manufacturing information for laminated composite structures. Computational structural analysis of typical aerospace composite structures coupled with experimental verification of the structural response.

title: MISSILE AERODYNAMICS
description: LEC. 3. Coreq. AERO 4140 . Aerodynamics of slender wing-body combinations, interference effects, linear and non-linear effects, applications to missile design and performance.

title: ROTARY WING AERODYNAMICS
description: LEC. 3. Aerodynamics and flight characteristics of rotary-wing aircraft.

title: COMPUTATIONAL FLUID DYNAMICS
description: LEC. 3. An introduction to finite-difference and finite-volume methods for solving partial differential equations of interest in fluid dynamics.

title: FLIGHT SIMULATION
description: LEC. 3. Time domain simulation of nonlinear, six-degree-of-freedom motion of flight vehicles. Development of modular digital simulations including vehicle models for aerodynamics and propulsion, control, guidance subsystems.

title: APPLIED ORBITAL MECHANICS
description: LEC. 3. Special perturbation techniques: N-body perturbations; general and restricted  three-body problems; preliminary orbit determination; C-W equations, targeting and rendezvous; constellation design; mission planning.

title: AEROACOUSTICS
description: LEC. 3. Pr. AERO 4140 or Departmental approval. Fundamental concepts in acoustics: decibel scales, sound propagation and measurement, plane and spherical waves, room acoustics, transmission and reflection, reverberant fields and noise assessment.  May count either AERO 5410 /5413 or AERO 6410 /6416.

title: PERTURBATION METHODS
description: LEC. 3. Pr. MATH 2650 . Departmental approval. Analytical solutions of nonlinear problems, ODES, PDEs, multiple scales, and transcendental equations in engineering, mathematics, and physics using both regular and singular perturbation methods.  May count either AERO/ MATH 5460 or AERO/ MATH 6460 .

title: ROCKET PROPULSION
description: LEC. 3. Analysis of the thermodynamics, gas dynamics and design of liquid and solid propellant rocket engines.

title: SPACE PROPULSION
description: LEC. 3. Pr. AERO 4510 . Analysis of space propulsion systems. Dynamics of electromagnetic systems, ion engines, photon drives, laser propulsion.

title: AEROSPACE APPLICATIONS OF COMPOSITE MATERIALS
description: LEC. 3. LAB. 3. Pr. AERO 3610 . Basic material and manufacturing information for laminated composite structures. Computational structural analysis of typical aerospace composite structures coupled with experimental verification of the structural response.

title: ADVANCED VISCOELASTICITY
description: DSL/LEC. An introduction to polymers and the theory of viscoelasticity. Topics include (1) a review of stress and strain analysis and measurement; (2) characteristics, applications, and properties of polymers; (3) polymerization and classification; (4) differential constitutive equations; (5) time and temperature behavior of polymers; (6) viscoelastic stress analysis; and (7) rate and time-dependent failure.

title: ADVANCED SUPERSONIC AERODYNAMICS
description: LEC. 3. Pr. AERO 4140 . A rigorous development of linearized and nonlinear fluid flow theories and application. Lifting surfaces, lifting bodies, duct flow, boundary layer effects, shock and expansion waves and method of characteristics.

title: DYNAMICS OF VISCOUS FLUIDS I
description: LEC. 3. Pr. AERO 7100 or AERO 7106. Exact solutions to the Navier Stokes equations. Exact and approximate solutions of the laminar boundary layer equations. Incompressible and compressible boundary layers in theory and experiment.

title: DYNAMICS OF VISCOUS FLUIDS II
description: LEC. 3. Pr. AERO 7120 or AERO 7126. Turbulent flows, the Reynolds stresses and turbulence modeling. Computation of incompressible and compressible turbulent boundary layers. Stability theory and transition.

title: ADVANCED COMPUTATIONAL FLUID DYNAMICS
description: LEC. 3. Pr. AERO 5140 or AERO 6140 . Advanced methods for solving problems in computational fluid dynamics.  Topics include: discretization approaches, implicit solution techniques, curvilinear coordinate systems, and upwind schemes.

title: COMPRESSIBLE FLUID DYNAMICS
description: LEC. 3. Pr. AERO 4140 . Departmental approval. An introduction to the fundamental of compressible fluid dynamics. Application of conservation of mass, momentum and energy for compressible flows. May count either AERO 7150 or AERO 7156.

title: PHYSICAL FOUNDATIONS OF TURBULENCE
description: LEC. 3. Pr. AERO 7120 or AERO 7126. Departmental approval. An introduction to turbulence using classical descriptions with a focus on the physics of turbulence phenomena.  May count either AERO 7160 or AERO 7166.

title: FUNDAMENTALS OF FLUIDS
description: LEC. 3. Introduction to principal concepts and methods of fluid dynamics; similarity and dimensional analysis; conservation of mass, momentum, and energy for flows in continuum; circulation and vorticity theorems; potential flow theory; introduction to some exact solutions based on simplifying assumptions for inviscid, viscous, unsteady, and compressible flow problems.

title: DYNAMICS OF FLIGHT
description: LEC. 3. Departmental approval. Development of specialized concepts and methods in dynamics applicable to the modeling of flight vehicle motion. Stability concepts and analysis of the stability of flight vehicle motions. Effects of variable mass and flexibility.

title: FLIGHT DYNAMICS OF HYPERVELOCITY VEHICLES
description: LEC. 3. Pr. AERO 7200 or AERO 7206. Departmental approval. Development of specialized concepts and methods in dynamics applicable to the modeling of hypersonic flight vehicle motion. Stability concepts and analysis of the stability of steady-state motions of very high speed flight vehicles.

title: SPACECRAFT ATTITUDE DYNAMICS AND CONTROL
description: LEC. 3. Pr. AERO 7200 or AERO 7206. Development of specialized concepts and methods in dynamics applicable to the modeling of spacecraft rotational motion.  Methods for controlling spacecraft attitude. Analysis of the attitude stability and controllability of spacecraft attitude motion. Department approval.

title: ORBIT DETERMINATION
description: LEC. 3. Pr. AERO 6330 or AERO 6336 or AERO 6230 or AERO 6236. Elements of orbit determination; least squares, minimum norm, minimum variance solutions; batch, sequential and extended sequential filters.

title: ADVANCED ORBITAL MECHANICS
description: LEC. 3. Pr. AERO 6330 or AERO 6336 or AERO 6230 or AERO 6236. Elements of time measurements, earth orientation/coordinate system; f and g series; Lambert's Problem; linear orbit theory and circumlunar trajectories.

title: OPTIMAL CONTROL OF AEROSPACE VEHICLES
description: LEC. 3. Principles of optimization; Pontryagin's principle; Linear quadratic regulator; Observers, state estimation, LQG problem. Optimal output feedback; Synthesis of flight control systems. AERO 3220 or equivalent.

title: ADVANCED TRAJECTORY OPTIMIZATION
description: LEC. 3. Departmental approval. This course reviews single- and multi-variable optimization techniques of deterministic continuous systems. A review of the necessary (KKT) and sufficient conditions for optimality is given. We will review principles of dynamic programming and the Hamilton-Jacobi-Bellman (HJB) equation. We will also review Linear Programming (LP) problems and Quadratic Programming (QP) problems. Application of the QP problems for minimum-snap trajectory optimization for path planning of quad-rotors is demonstrated. We will also introduce basic concepts of convex optimization, which uses many of the concepts introduced in the LP and QP problems. The final project is to solve a trajectory optimization problem using successive linearization, which is a widely used technique for converting non-convex problems to convex ones.

title: LIGHT-FIELD IMAGING
description: LEC. 3. Pr. AERO 7160 or AERO 7166. Departmental approval. An introduction to light-field imaging.  Topics include light parameterization, light field cameras, computational photography and Fourier slice photography theorem.  May count either AERO 7410 or AERO 7416.

title: PARTICE IMAGE VELOCIMETRY
description: LEC. 3. Pr. AERO 7120 or AERO 7126. Departmental approval. An introduction to particle image velocimetry and it variations including conventional planar  PIV, stereo PIV, stereo-PIV and torno-PIV.  May count either AERO 7420 or AERO 7426.

title: AEROSPACE ENGINEERING ANALYSIS
description: LEC. 3. Analysis and techniques for solving ordinary and partial differential equations common in Aerospace applications.

title: ADVANCED PERTURBATION METHODS
description: LEC. 3. Departmental approval. Solutions of nonlinear problems and integrals using WKB, Rayleigh-Janzen, Generalized Scales, Latta, van der Pol, Watson, Laplace, Adomian, homotopy, Pade, Liouville-Green and Burmann transformations.  May count either AERO 7460 /7466 or MATH 7460 /7466.

title: THRUST GENERATION
description: LEC. 3. Pr. AERO 4510 . Aerothermodynamics of propulsion. Selected topics in gas dynamics, thermodynamics, and heat transfer as applied to airbreathing and space propulsion.

title: ADVANCED AIR-BREATHING PROPULSION
description: LEC. 3. Topics emphasizing interaction between external aerodynamics and performance of airbreathing jet engines. Performance optimization of ramjet, turbojet, and turbofan engines. Component matching.

title: AEROTHERMCHEM OF PROPULSION
description: LEC. 3. Aerothermodynamics of compressible flow, chemical propellant characteristics, heat transfer in fluid flow, statistical gas dynamics, kinetic theory of gases.

title: AEROSPACE SOLID MECHANICS
description: LEC. 3. An introduction to solid mechanics concepts with aerospace engineering applications. The course develops equations of motions from conservation laws and introduces constitutive equations from linearized continuum mechanics perspective for aerospace related applications. Topics include elastostatic solutions, elastodynamic solutions and plasticity.

title: AEROSPACE COMPUTATIONAL STRUCTURAL ANALYSIS: STATIC STRUCTURES
description: LEC. 3. Pr. AERO 4620 . Departmental approval. Advanced techniques for the numerical solution of static elastic and plastic problems, including two and three dimensional solutions.

title: AEROSPACE COMPUTATIONAL STRUCTURAL ANALYSIS: STRUCTURAL DYNAMICS
description: LEC. 3. Pr. AERO 4630 . Departmental approval. Advanced techniques for the numerical solution to problems in structural dynamics, including steady state and transient response of two-and three-dimensional structures.

title: SEMINAR
description: SEM. 0.  SU. Weekly lectures on current developments in aerospace sciences by staff members, graduate students, and visiting scientists and engineers. Course may be repeated for a maximum of 1 credit hours.

title: SPECIAL TOPICS IN AEROSPACE ENGINEERING
description: DSL.  Course may be repeated for a maximum of 9 credit hours.

title: AEROSPACE ENGINEERING PROJECT
description: LEC. 3.  SU. Departmental approval. Intended for students in the MAE program. On or off-campus project. The nature of the project is to be determined by the student's major professor. Approval of the project and its final written report by the student's advisory committee is required. Course may be repeated with change in topic.

title: RESEARCH AND THESIS
description: DSL/MST. Credit hours to be arranged. Course may be repeated with change in topics.

title: RESEARCH AND DISSERTATION
description: DSL/DSR. Individual doctoral dissertation research. May be repeated for credit. Course may be repeated with change in topics.

title: PRINCIPLES OF FINANCIAL ACCOUNTING
description: LEC. 3. Basic accounting principles with focus on preparation and use of financial statements. Credit will not be given for both ACCT 2110 and ACCT 2810 . Sophomore standing.

title: HONORS PRINCIPLES OF FINANCIAL ACCOUNTING
description: LEC. 3. Pr. Honors College. Basic accounting principles with focus on preparation and use of financial statements. Sophomore standing.

title: PRINCIPLES OF MANAGERIAL ACCOUNTING
description: LEC. 3. Pr. ACCT 2110 or ACCT 2113 or ACCT 2117 . Emphasis on cost accounting, budgeting, and decision making using managerial accounting information. Sophomore standing.

title: BUSINESS LAW
description: LEC. 3. Introduction to contracts, sales, torts, ethics and the judicial system.  Focus is on the business environment.

title: HONORS BUSINESS LAW
description: LEC. 3. Pr. Honors College. Introduction to contracts, sales, torts, ethics and the judicial system. Focus is on the business environment.

title: FUNDAMENTALS OF ACCOUNTING
description: LEC. 3. Principles of financial and managerial accounting. Not open to undergraduates majoring in Business. Credit will not be given for both ACCT 2110 and ACCT 2810 .

title: HONORS FUNDAMENTALS OF ACCOUNTING
description: LEC. 3. Pr. Honors College. Principles of financial and managerial accounting. Not open to undergraduates majoring in Business. Credit will not be given for both ACCT 2110 and ACCT 2810 .

title: ACCOUNTING FOR DECISION MAKING
description: LEC. 3. Pr. ACCT 2110 or ACCT 2113 or ACCT 2117 . A study of how cost data for products, projects, or services are recorded, analyzed, and used for decision making. Junior standing applies to ACCT 3210 . ACCT 3210 is limited to students in the BSBA Online completer program.

title: BUSINESS PROCESSES AND INTERNAL CONTROLS
description: LEC. 3. Pr. ACCT 2110 or ACCT 2113 or ACCT 2117 . Developing knowledge of business processes, accounting for those business processes, and the internal controls surrounding such processes, both in a manual and computerized environment. Limited to students in the BSBA Online completer program. Credit will not be given for both ACCT 3310 and ACCT 3520.

title: PROFESSIONAL DEVELOPMENT IN ACCOUNTANCY
description: LEC. 1.  SU. Pr. (P/C ACCT 3110 or P/C ACCT 3113 or P/C ACCT 5110 ) and P/C BUSI 2010 . Career planning and preparation for transition from university student to accounting professional.

title: DIRECTED STUDIES
description: IND.  SU. Departmental approval. Advanced individual research and study in accounting under the direction of a faculty member. Course may be repeated for a maximum of 6 credit hours.

title: ACCOUNTING INTERNSHIP
description: DSL/LEC.  SU. Internship opportunity with an accounting firm, corporation, or governmental entity. Course may be repeated for a maximum of 6 credit hours.

title: INTERMEDIATE ACCOUNTING I
description: LEC. 3. Pr. ACCT 2110 or ACCT 2113 or ACCT 2117 . Accounting principles and theory including accounting for current assets, liabilities, and investments. Junior standing applies to ACCT 5110 . ACCT 5113 is limited to students accepted to online accounting program.

title: INTERMEDIATE ACCOUNTING II
description: LEC. 3. Pr. ACCT 3110 or ACCT 3113 or ACCT 5110 . Grade of C or better. Continuation of ACCT 5110 , with emphasis on fixed assets, capital structure, and cash flows. Junior standing applies to ACCT 5120 . ACCT 5123 is limited to students accepted to online accounting program.

title: ADVANCED ACCOUNTING TOPICS
description: LEC. 3. Pr. ACCT 3120 or ACCT 3123 or ACCT 5120 . Emphasis on advanced accounting topics including business combinations, foreign currency transactions, derivatives, and other advanced financial topics.

title: SPECIAL TOPICS IN ACCOUNTING
description: LEC. 3. Pr. ACCT 3120 or ACCT 3123 or ACCT 5120 . A study of current issues in accounting theory and practice. Topics include regulations and economic and technological developments.

title: CURRENT ISSUES IN ACCOUNTING
description: LEC. 3. Pr. P/C ACCT 5110 . Departmental approval. Examination of current issues in the accounting profession.

title: COST ACCOUNTING
description: LEC. 3. Pr. ACCT 2110 or ACCT 2113 or ACCT 2117 . A study of how cost data for products, projects, or services are recorded, analyzed, and used for decision making. Junior standing applies to ACCT 5210 .

title: AUDITING & ASSURANCE SERVICES
description: LEC. 3. Pr. ACCT 3110 or ACCT 5110 . Principles of auditing standards, ethics, controls, evidence, sampling, and audit reports.

title: INCOME TAX I
description: LEC. 3. Pr. ACCT 3110 or ACCT 3113 or ACCT 5110 . Principles of federal taxation as it applies to individuals and property transactions.

title: INCOME TAX II
description: LEC. 3. Pr. ACCT 4410 or ACCT 4413 or ACCT 5410 . Tax accounting for individuals, partnerships, corporations, estates, and trusts.  Extensive use of a tax-service program. ACCT 5423 is limited to students accepted to online accounting program.

title: ACCOUNTING INFORMATION SYSTEMS
description: LEC. 3. Pr. P/C ACCT 3110 or P/C ACCT 3113 or P/C ACCT 5110 . Developing knowledge of business processes, accounting for those business processes, and the internal controls surrounding such processes, both in a manual and computerized environment.

title: ACCOUNTING ANALYTICS
description: LEC. 3. Pr. (ACCT 3110 or ACCT 3113 or ACCT 5110 ) and ( CTCT 3250 or CTCT 3253) and (P/C ACCT 3520 or P/C ACCT 3523 or P/C ACCT 5510 ). Students will learn to analyze data and solve accounting based problems using advanced spreadsheet techniques, database management systems and other analysis tools.

title: GOVERNMENTAL AND NOT-FOR-PROFIT ACCOUNTING
description: LEC. 3. Pr. ACCT 3120 or ACCT 3123 or ACCT 5120 . Accounting for governmental and not-for-profit entities. Focus on effective use of resources.

description: LEC. 3. Pr. P/C ACCT 5110 . Departmental approval. Accounting issues concerning international business activity.

description: LEC. 3. Pr. ACCT 3120 or ACCT 3123 or ACCT 5120 . Analyzing the impact of corporate governance and accounting ethics on business transactions.

description: LEC. 3. Pr. ACCT 4410 or ACCT 4413 or ACCT 5410 or ACCT 6410 or ACCT 6416. Tax accounting for individuals, partnerships, corporations, estates and trusts. Extensive use of a tax-service program.

description: LEC. 3. Pr. ACCT 3120 or ACCT 3123 or ACCT 5120 or ACCT 6120 . Departmental approval. An evaluation, critique, and application of financial accounting theory to current reporting problems using current research tools and resources.

title: FINANCIAL ANALYSIS & VALUATION
description: LEC. 3. Pr. ACCT 3120 or ACCT 3123 or ACCT 5120 or ACCT 6120 . Financial analysis to support managerial, investor, and creditor decision-making, forecasting financial statements and earnings, and applying valuation models to accounting measurement and investment decisions.

title: ACCOUNTING FOR DECISION MAKING AND CONTROL
description: LEC. 3. Pr. ACCT 3210 or ACCT 3213 or ACCT 5210 or ACCT 5213 or ACCT 6210 or ACCT 6216. Departmental approval. Relationship between management accounting and information systems and analysis of costs.

title: ADVANCED AUDITING AND ASSURANCE SERVICES
description: LEC. 3. Pr. ACCT 4310 or ACCT 4313 or ACCT 5300 or ACCT 5303 or ACCT 6300 or ACCT 6306. Advanced topics in auditing and assurance services.

title: FRAUD EXAMINATION
description: LEC. 3. Pr. ACCT 4310 or ACCT 4313 or ACCT 5300 or ACCT 5303 or ACCT 6300 or ACCT 6306. Learning how and why occupational fraud is committed and how fraudulent conduct is deterred, investigated, and resolved.

title: FEDERAL TAX RESEARCH
description: LEC. 3. Pr. ACCT 5420 or ACCT 5423 or P/C ACCT 6420 or ACCT 6426. Departmental approval. Sources of authority used in federal tax research and survey of tax policy issues.

description: LEC. 3. Pr. ANSC 1000 and ( ECON 2020 or ECON 2023 or ECON 2027 ). Practical concepts of equine marketing including evaluating the horse, assessing the market, targeting customers, and presenting the horse.

description: LAB. 4. Instruction for the evaluation of meat carcasses, cuts, and products from cattle, swine, sheep, and goats. This course  is required for participation in the meat judging program but is open to all students.

description: LEC. 3. LAB. 2. Pr. CHEM 1040 and ( BIOL 1030 or BIOL 1037 or POUL 3150 ). Principles and practice of animal nutrition, nutrient contents of feedstuff, and diet formulation.

title: VALUE BASED MARKETING OF LIVESTOCK
description: LEC. 2. LAB. 2. Livestock grading standards and their application to carcasses of meat producing animals, concepts and principles of marketing, advertising, promotion and sales of commercial livestock.

title: MODERN LIVESTOCK SYSTEMS

description: LEC. 3. LAB. 2. Pr. ( ANSC 3400 or ANSC 3420 ) and ANSC 3500 and ANSC 3600 . Overview of the beef cattle industry. description: LEC. 3. LAB. 2. Pr. ( ANSC 3400 or ANSC 3420 or ANSC 4450 ) and ANSC 3500 and ANSC 3600 . Practical application and integration of nutrition, breeding, reproduction, selection, herd health, and management for efficient horse production.

description: LEC. 2. LAB. 2. Pr. ANSC 3410 or ANSC 3400 . Principles of digestive physiology, nutrition, and metabolic disorders unique to various classes of horses.

description: LAB. 2. Pr. COMM 1000 or COMM 1003. Methods for enhancing effective discourse concerning issues facing the livestock industry.

description: LEC. 3. Pr. BIOL 1030 or BIOL 1037 or BIOL 3200 . description: LEC. 3. Explores standards of practice in forensic anthropology and analysis of case studies.

title: PEOPLES AND CULTURES OF ASIA
description: LEC. 3. Any Social Science Core course.  Introduction to the traditions, religions, histories, and nation-states of the people of Asia, using a cultural approach.

title: ANTHROPOLOGY OF THE AFRICAN DIASPORA
description: LEC. 3. Any Social Science Core course. Anthropological perspectives on African Diasporas. Diaspora.  Archaeological, ethnohistorical, and contemporary research exploring identity, symbols, power, and social relations in the lives of enslaved Africans and descendants in the Caribbean, Latin America and North America.

title: WORLD PREHISTORY
description: LEC. 3. Explore broad patterns in human prehistory over the past 10,000 years, including the origins of culture, religion, domestication and agriculture, writing, cities, and states.

title: CULTURE, MARRIAGE, AND THE FAMILY
description: LEC. 3. Role and meaning of kinship and its universal and particularistic features in human society.

title: MAGIC, MYTH, AND RITUAL: ANTHROPOLOGY OF RELIGION
description: LEC. Pr. P/C ANTH 1000 . This course explores religious beliefs and practices around the world from an anthropological perspective. Our focus will be on how anthropologists study religion and how religious beliefs, practices, and practitioners are understood in their historical and cultural contexts.

title: LANGUAGE AND CULTURE
description: LEC. 3. Pr. ANTH 1000 or ANTH 1003 or ANTH 1007 . Contemporary perspectives on cultural and linguistic anthropology, emphasizing language acquisition and culture change; language as socially charged; performance; status and inequality; and the study of globally diverse linguistic communities of practice.

title: ANTHROPOLOGY AND FICTION
description: LEC. Inter-disciplinary study of fiction from an anthropological perspective.

title: ANTHROPOLOGY OF GENDER
description: LEC. 3. Pr. P/C ANTH 1000 or P/C ANTH 1003 or P/C ANTH 1007 or P/C WMST 2100 or P/C WMST 2103. Gender relations and representations in different cultures, historical periods, and discourses.

title: BIOLOGICAL ANTHROPOLOGY
description: LEC. 3. Pr. ANTH 1000 or ANTH 1003 or ANTH 1007 or BIOL 1000 or BIOL 1010 . Overview of biological anthropology, including evolutionary theory and genetics, primatology, human origins, and biological variation of contemporary human populations. Concepts will be applied during in-class exercises and discussions.

title: APPLIED & PRACTICING ANTHROPOLOGY
description: LEC. 3. Pr. ANTH 1000 or ANTH 1003 or ANTH 1007 . Explores application and practice of anthropology in settings such as cultural resource management, museums, social and environmental policy, and healthcare. This course emphasizes how careers in anthropology contribute to resolving contemporary social problems.

title: ARCHAEOLOGY
description: LEC. 3. Pr. ANTH 1000 or ANTH 1003 or ANTH 1007 . Archaeology is the study of human societies based on the material remains they left behind. This course explores the history, theories, methods, and applications of archaeology.

title: MEDICAL ANTHROPOLOGY
description: LEC. 3. Any Social Science Core course. Explores biological and cultural dimensions of global health from an anthropological perspective. Topics include the political economy of health, gendered health disparities, cross-cultural healing traditions, pluralistic medical systems, and evolutionary medicine.

title: FORENSIC ANTHROPOLOGY II: ADVANCED METHODS, THEORY, AND CASE STUDIES
description: LAB. Pr. ANTH 2610 . Forensic Anthropology II is a hybrid seminar and lab course that teaches practical applications and methods in forensic anthropology through the exploration of case studies and laboratory exercises.

title: ENVIRONMENTAL ANTHROPOLOGY
description: LEC. 3. Problems in environmental anthropology including ethnoecology, cultural ecology, political ecology and environmentalism.

title: ARCHAEOLOGY OF NORTH AMERICA
description: LEC. 3. Pr. ANTH 1000 or ANTH 1003 or ANTH 1007 . Explores archaeological evidence for the history of indigenous peoples in North America during the past 10,000 years.

title: ARCHAEOLOGY OF THE SOUTHEASTERN AND MIDWESTERN U.S.
description: LEC. 3. Pr. ANTH 1000 or ANTH 1003 or ANTH 1007 . Diversity and complexity of late prehistoric cultures of the Southeastern and Midwestern United States.

title: BIOARCHAEOLOGY
description: LEC. 3. Pr. ( ANTH 1000 or ANTH 1003 or ANTH 1007 ). Archaeologically-derived skeletal remains provide essential information for reconstructing broad patterns of human health and behavior over time. Students will learn to apply methods and theory in social bioarchaeology to understand demography, diet, disease, and physical activity in past populations.

title: CURATION
description: LEC. 3. Pr. ANTH 1000 or ANTH 1003 or ANTH 1007 . History, legislation, and ethical concerns associated with the accumulation and curation of archaeological collections.

title: THEORIES OF CULTURE IN ANTHROPOLOGY
description: LEC. 3. Pr. ANTH 1000 or ANTH 1003 or ANTH 1007 . Major thinkers in anthropology and their theoretical models considered in historical perspective.

title: ARCHAEOLOGICAL FIELD SCHOOL
description: LEC. 1. LAB. 2. Pr. ANTH 1000 or ANTH 1003 or ANTH 1007 . Field methods, including archaeological surveying and excavation procedures at selected locations. Course may be repeated for a maximum of 6 credit hours.

title: BIOLOGICAL ANTHROPOLOGY LABORATORY PRACTICUM
description: LEC. 1. LAB. 2. Pr. ( ANTH 1000 or ANTH 1003 or ANTH 1007 ). Investigation of specific biological anthropology problem or problems, involving students in laboratory techniques and research.

title: INTERNSHIP IN ANTHROPOLOGY
description: AAB/INT. 1-3. Pr. ANTH 1000 or ANTH 1003 or ANTH 1007 . Internship for practical work with anthropological/archaeological collections or anthropological research in a professional setting, including federal or state agencies, NGOs, NPOs, community and voluntary organizations, and industry (e.g., healthcare and medicine, advertising/media, architecture/design, high technology, Cultural Resource Management and historic preservation, etc.). Course must be approved by the faculty advisor and department. Internship must be a supervised, closely monitored work experience, appropriate to the major. Course may be repeated for a maximum of 6 credit hours.

title: CULTURAL ANTHROPOLOGY FIELD SCHOOL
description: FLD. 3. Pr. ANTH 1000 or ANTH 1003 or ANTH 1007 . This practice anthropology course examines the relationship between culture, community, and environment in a particular field setting, in the United States or abroad.

title: ARCHAEOLOGY LABORATORY PRACTICUM
description: LEC. 1. LAB. 2. Pr. ANTH 1000 or ANTH 1003 or ANTH 1007 . Analysis, preservation, cataloging, and restoration of archaeological materials.  May count either ANTH 4940 or ANTH 3910.

title: SPECIAL PROBLEMS
description: LEC. 3. Pr. ANTH 1000 or ANTH 1003 or ANTH 1007 . Specific topics in anthropology not covered in other courses. Course may be repeated for a maximum of 6 credit hours.

title: HONORS SPECIAL PROBLEMS
description: IND. 3. Pr. Honors College. ANTH 1000 . Departmental approval. Specific topics in anthropology not covered in other courses. Course may be repeated for a maximum of 3 credit hours.

title: HONORS THESIS
description: IND. Pr. Honors College. ANTH 1000 . Departmental approval..  Course may be repeated for a maximum of 3 credit hours.

title: GENDER DEVELOPMENT AND CULTURE
description: LEC. 3. Pr. ANTH 1000 or ANTH 1003 or ANTH 1007 . or junior standing. Role of gender and culture in Third World development from an anthropological perspective.

title: CULTURE, MEDICINE, AND POWER
description: LEC. 3. Pr. ANTH 1000 or ANTH 1003 or ANTH 1007 . or junior standing. Power in the context of illness and healing at local, national, and international levels.

title: CRITIQUE OF DEVELOPMENT
description: LEC. 3. Pr. ANTH 3700 . Meanings and structures of national and international development.

title: DIRECTED STUDIES
description: IND. Pr. ANTH 1000 or ANTH 1003 or ANTH 1007 . Directed study course in anthropology that allows students to explore concepts not covered in other courses. Course may be repeated for a maximum of 6 credit hours.

title: SPECIAL TOPICS IN ANTHROPOLOGY
description: LEC. 3. Pr. ANTH 1000 or ANTH 1003 or ANTH 1007 . Examination of a specific problem in ethnographic methods, theory, and cultural analysis.

title: GENDER DEVELOPMENT AND CULTURE
description: LEC. 3. Role of gender and culture in Third World economic development from an anthropological perspective.

title: CULTURE MEDICINE AND POWER
description: LEC. 3. Power in the context of illness and healing at local, national, and international levels.

title: DIRECTED STUDY
description: IND. Directed study course in anthropology that allows students to explore concepts not covered in other courses. Course may be repeated for a maximum of 6 credit hours.

title: SPECIAL TOPICS IN ANTHROPOLOGY
description: LEC. 3. Pr. ANTH 1000 or ANTH 1003 or ANTH 1007 . Examination of a specific problem in ethnographic methods, theory, and cultural analysis.

title: BASIC PAINTING FOR NON MAJORS
description: STU. 6. Instruction in painting concepts, materials, and techniques. Oil and water-based paints and other media are used to explore a variety of approaches and subject matter. Not open to ARTF, ARTH, and ATLA majors.

title: DRAWING I
description: AAB/STU. 6. Basic drawing with emphasis on accurate observation, pictorial organization, and the depiction of space; development of drawing skills using various black and white media.

title: STUDIO FOUNDATIONS I: MATERIALS AND CONCEPTS
description: STU. 6. An introductory course on the application of two-dimensional and three-dimensional art materials and conceptual development.

title: STUDIO FOUNDATIONS II: PROCESS AND CONCEPT
description: STU. 6. Pr. P/C ARTS 1210 . An introductory art course focusing on in-depth creative research in conjunction with artmaking.

title: INTRODUCTION TO DIGITAL ART
description: STU. 6. An introduction to the skills and concepts of digital art such as imaging, time, and 3D modeling using computer-based tools and techniques.

title: ORIENTATION TO STUDIO ART FOR THE MAJOR
description: LEC. 0.  SU. Introduction to the BA and BFA studio arts major, photo documentation, and portfolio development.

title: LOOKING AT ART: APPROACHES TO INTERPRETATION
description: LEC. 3. Introduces the fundamental structures of the art world and multiple approaches to looking at and responding to art.

title: INTRODUCTION TO ART HISTORY
description: LEC. 3. This introduction to global art history teaches the basic concepts of visual analysis by discussing the historical, social, and political contexts of major themes in art history. Specific topics and emphases vary by instructor.

title: HONORS INTRODUCTION TO ART HISTORY
description: LEC. 3. Pr. Honors College. This introduction to global art history teaches the basic concepts of visual analysis by discussing the historical, social, and political contexts of major themes in art history. Specific topics and emphases vary by instructor.

title: FOUNDATIONS OF ART HISTORY I
description: LEC. 3. A history of art from ancient cultures to approximately 1300 CE, with an introduction to basic art historical research and writing skills.

title: FOUNDATIONS OF ART HISTORY II
description: LEC. 3. A history of art from approximately 1300 CE to the contemporary period, with an introduction to basic art historical research and writing skills.

title: SPECIAL TOPICS IN STUDIO ART AND ART HISTORY
description: LEC. 3. Topics in studio art and art history.  Focus will vary according to the instructor. Course may be repeated for a maximum of 6 credit hours.

title: 2-D ANIMATION
description: STU. 6. Pr. ( ARTS 2100 or ARTS 2150 ) and ARTS 1110 and ARTS 1210 and ARTS 1230 . Introduction to the fundamental principles of animation and storytelling in 2-D traditional and digital formats.

title: B.F.A. PORTFOLIO REVIEW
description: IND.  SU. Pr. ARTS 1110 and ARTS 1210 and ARTS 1220 and ARTS 1230 and ARTS 1250 and ARTS 2100 and ARTS 2150 . Preparation, presentation, and faculty advisory review of a portfolio of artworks, a statement of academic and career goals, and a declaration of a BFA concentration area. Completed by ARTF students after 24 credit hours of ARTS courses.

title: FIGURE DRAWING I
description: STU. 6. Pr. ARTS 1110 and ARTS 1210 and ( ARTS 2100 or ARTS 2150 ). The human figure as form and as compositional element. Measuring and sighting for proportion. Drawing from casts, skeletons, and nude models.

title: 3-D ANIMATION
description: STU. 6. Pr. ( ARTS 2100 and ARTS 2150 ) and ARTS 1220 and ARTS 3020 and ARTS 3110 . Introduction to the fundamental principles of 3D modeling and animation.

title: THEMES IN CONTEMPORARY ANIMATION
description: STU. 6. Pr. ( ARTS 2100 and ARTS 2150 ) and ARTS 1220 and ARTS 3120 . Creative exploration of varying topics in contemporary animation. Studio work supplemented with lectures, critiques, and reading. Course may be repeated for a maximum of 6 credit hours.

title: ADVANCED DRAWING I
description: STU. 6. Pr. ( ARTS 2100 or ARTS 2150 ) and ARTS 1220 and ARTS 1230 and ARTS 3110 . Concepts, materials and techniques with emphasis on the development of a personal vision and individual approach. Nude models may be used.

title: ADVANCED DRAWING II
description: STU. 6. Pr. ARTS 3140 and ( ARTS 2100 and ARTS 2150 ). Medium and subject determined by student with approval of instructor. Emphasis on strengthening the student's aesthetic awareness and technical skills.

title: FIGURE DRAWING II
description: STU. 6. Pr. ( ARTS 2100 and ARTS 2150 ) and ARTS 1220 and ARTS 1230 and ARTS 3110 . Continued study of life-drawing from nude models and casts. Materials and techniques emphasize building observational drawing skills and strengthening aesthetic awareness. Repeatable up to 6 hours. Course may be repeated for a maximum of 6 credit hours.

title: INTRODUCTION TO PHOTOGRAPHY
description: STU. 3. Pr. ( ARTS 2100 or ARTS 2150 ) and ARTS 1110 and ARTS 1210 . Departmental approval. Fine art photographic concepts and techniques including camera operation, tonal control of black and white prints, presentations of historical and contemporary photography.

title: DIGITAL AND COLOR PHOTOGRAPHY
description: AAB/STU. 6. Pr. ( ARTS 2100 and ARTS 2150 ) and ARTS 1220 and ARTS 1230 and ARTS 3210 . Departmental approval. Concepts and practices of contemporary art photography including digital production techniques and color photographic theory.

title: INTERMEDIATE PHOTOGRAPHY
description: STU. 6. Pr. ( ARTS 2100 and ARTS 2150 ) and ARTS 1220 and ARTS 1230 and ARTS 3210 . Departmental approval. Intermediate study of photographic processes with emphasis on technique, classroom craftsmanship, medium and large camera formats, approaches to content and researching concepts to inform studio production.

title: PAINTING I
description: STU. 6. Pr. ( ARTS 2100 or ARTS 2150 ) and ARTS 1110 and ARTS 1210 . Instruction in painting concepts, materials, and methods.

title: PAINTING II
description: AAB/STU. 6. Pr. ( ARTS 2100 and ARTS 2150 ) and ARTS 1220 and ARTS 1230 and ARTS 3310 . Departmental approval. Instruction in painting concepts, materials, and techniques with emphasis on the development of technical skills and a personal vision and individual approach.

title: PAINTING III
description: AAB/STU. 6. Pr. ARTS 3140 and ARTS 3320 or Departmental approval. Medium and subject determined by student and instructor. Emphasis on strengthening aesthetic awareness and technical skills.

title: PRINTMAKING: RELIEF
description: STU. 6. Pr. ( ARTS 2100 or ARTS 2150 ) and ARTS 1110 and ARTS 1210 . Introduction to relief printmaking. Studio work supplemented with lectures, critiques, and readings.

title: PRINTMAKING: INTAGLIO
description: STU. 6. Pr. ( ARTS 2100 and ARTS 2150 ) and ARTS 1220 and ARTS 1230 and ( ARTS 3410 or ARTS 3430 ) or Departmental approval. Introduction to intaglio printmaking.  Studio work with lectures, critiques, and readings.

title: PRINTMAKING: SCREEN-PRINTING
description: STU. 6. Pr. ( ARTS 2100 or ARTS 2150 ) and ARTS 1110 and ARTS 1210 and ARTS 1230 . Departmental approval. Introduction to water based screen-printing.  Studio work supplemented with lectures, critiques, and reading.

title: INTRODUCTION TO SCULPTURE
description: STU. 6. Pr. ( ARTS 2100 or ARTS 2150 ) and ARTS 1110 and ARTS 1210 . Introduction to wood working, metal fabrication, casting, and additional materials, processes, and issues involved in mixed media sculpture production. Department approval.

title: INSTALLATION ART
description: STU. 6. Pr. ARTS 2100 and ARTS 2150 and ARTS 1220 and ARTS 1230 and ARTS 3510 . Investigation of the methods, theory, and technologies involved in contemporary indoor and outdoor installations and site-specific intervention. Readings and discussions on relevant sculptural practices. Department approval.

title: CASTING AND FOUNDRY
description: STU. 6. Pr. ( ARTS 2100 and ARTS 2150 ) and ARTS 1220 and ARTS 1230 and ARTS 3510 . Instruction in mold making, casting, papermaking, and figure modeling as it applies to metal, plaster, and additional materials. Department approval.

title: COLONIAL LATIN AMERICAN ART
description: LEC. 3. Pr. ARTS 2100 and ARTS 2150 . Study of the major art and architectural traditions of Latin America from the early 16th to the early 19th centuries.

title: ART OF THE ANCIENT NEAR EAST
description: LEC. 3. Pr. ARTS 2100 and ARTS 2150 . This course examines the visual arts and architecture of the Near East within their social and historical contexts. Departmental Approval needed.

title: ANCIENT GREEK ART
description: LEC. 3. Pr. ARTS 2100 and ARTS 2150 . This course examines the visual arts and architecture of ancient Greece (Early Bronze Age to Hellenistic Period) in their social and historical contexts. Departmental Approval needed.

title: HISTORY OF PHOTOGRAPHY
description: LEC. 3. Pr. ( ARTS 2100 and ARTS 2150 ) or Departmental approval. Introduction to the history and theory of photography from its 19th-century origins to contemporary global practices.

title: EIGHTEENTH-CENTURY ART IN EUROPE
description: LEC. 3. Pr. ( ARTS 2100 and ARTS 2150 ) or Departmental approval. Study of the architecture, painting, and sculpture in 18th-century Europe.

title: CONSTRUCTING RACE IN THE VISUAL ARTS
description: LEC. 3. Pr. ( ARTS 2100 and ARTS 2150 ) or Departmental approval. Study of the role of visual imagery in inscribing and. challenging racial hierarchies in the history of art.

title: 20TH-CENTURY ART II: 1945-2000
description: LEC. 3. Pr. ( ARTS 2100 and ARTS 2150 ). An introduction to the artists, movements, institutions, concepts, and themes of late 20th-century art.

title: ARTS OF AFRICA
description: LEC. 3. Pr. ( ARTS 2100 and ARTS 2150 ). Introduction to the art, artists, themes and issues in African art from the pre-colonial period to the contemporary era.

title: ART OF THE UNITED STATES
description: LEC. 3. Pr. ( ARTS 2100 and ARTS 2150 ). Study of architecture, painting, and sculpture from colonial to recent times. Selected movements and works are considered in relationship both to European and to indigenous conditions and attitudes.

title: ANCIENT ART OF THE WEST
description: LEC. 3. Pr. ( ARTS 2100 and ARTS 2150 ) or Departmental approval. Examination of major art traditions of the ancient world, including for example: Egypt, Near East, Aegean, Greece, and Rome.

title: MEDIEVAL ART OF THE WEST
description: LEC. 3. Pr. ( ARTS 2100 and ARTS 2150 ). Study of  major art traditions of the West from the fall of Rome to CE 1400, with a selective focus on the major art traditions, including Migration period, Carolingian, Ottonian, Romanesque, Gothic, and Italo-Byzantine.

title: RENAISSANCE ART IN ITALY
description: LEC. 3. Pr. ( ARTS 2100 and ARTS 2150 ). Study of the architecture, painting, and sculpture of the 15th and 16th centuries in Italy.

title: SEVENTEENTH-CENTURY ART IN EUROPE
description: LEC. 3. Pr. ( ARTS 2100 and ARTS 2150 ) or Departmental approval. Study of the  architecture, painting, and sculpture of 17th-century Europe.

title: 19TH CENTURY ART
description: LEC. 3. Pr. ( ARTS 2100 and ARTS 2150 ). Introduction to major art movements from Neo-Classicism to Post-Impressionism and Art Nouveau.

title: 20TH CENTURY ART I 1900–1945
description: LEC. 3. Pr. ARTS 2100 and ARTS 2150 . Study of major developments in painting, sculpture, and architecture in Europe and the United States from 1900 to 1945. Departmental Approval.

title: MESOAMERICAN ART
description: LEC. 3. Pr. ( ARTS 2100 and ARTS 2150 ). Study of the major art and architectural traditions of Mesoamerica from 1800 BCE until 1519 CE.

title: ARTS OF ASIA
description: LEC. 3. Pr. ( ARTS 2100 and ARTS 2150 ). Introduction to major art traditions of Asia from the beginnings to the present.

title: ISSUES AND CRITICISM IN CONTEMPORARY ART
description: LEC. 3. Pr. ( ARTS 2100 and ARTS 2150 ). and one 3000-level art history class or Departmental approval. Readings and discussions about contemporary art.

title: GENDER AND THE VISUAL ARTS
description: LEC. 3. Pr. ( ARTS 2100 and ARTS 2150 ) or Departmental approval. Introduction to gender issues in the visual arts in historical and contemporary contexts. Examines the cultural notions of both masculine and feminine gender roles at play in works of art and explores key issues that have affected women's production of works of art in the past and present.

title: INTRODUCTION TO WHEEL-THROWN CERAMICS
description: STU. 6. Pr. ( ARTS 2100 or ARTS 2150 ) and ARTS 1110 and ARTS 1210 . Departmental approval. Introduction to wheel-thrown pottery. Presentation of historical and contemporary contexts for fine arts ceramics. Work with glazes and firing.

title: INTRODUCTION TO HAND-BUILT CERAMICS
description: STU. 6. Pr. ( ARTS 2100 or ARTS 2150 ) and ARTS 1110 and ARTS 1210 . Departmental approval. Introduction to handforming methods for sculpture and vessel forms in clay. Work with glazes and firing.

title: INTERMEDIATE CERAMICS
description: STU. 6. Pr. ( ARTS 2100 and ARTS 2150 ) and ARTS 1220 and ARTS 1230 and ( ARTS 3820 or ARTS 3840 ). Departmental approval. Individual approaches to ceramic sculpture and vessel forms, techniques building on previous experience with hand-building and/or wheel-throwing, with emphasis on stylistic and conceptual concerns.

title: INTERNSHIP IN STUDIO ART/ART HISTORY
description: AAB/PRA. 15.  SU. Junior standing and 3.0 GPA in major and completion of at least two 3000-level courses in ARTF, ARTH, or ATLA major. Internships appropriate to the major with a departmental-approved sponsor providing hands-on, practical learning experiences in a professional setting.

title: STUDIO ART ABROAD
description: AAB/LEC. 6. Studio art taught on site in foreign destination.

title: ART HISTORY ABROAD
description: LEC. 3. Art History taught on site in foreign destination.

title: SEMINAR IN PRE-MODERN ART HISTORY
description: SEM. 3. 6 hours of 3000-level art history courses. Or departmental approval. Examination of varying topics in art history from the ancient era until 1750 CE. Course may be repeated for a maximum of 6 credit hours.

title: ADVANCED ANIMATION
description: STU. 6. Pr. ARTS 3130 . An advanced investigation of theory, history, contemporary practice, and techniques through research and studio practice. Emphasis on mature creative expression and production as an animator. Course may be repeated for a maximum of 6 credit hours.

title: SEMINAR IN MODERN AND CONTEMPORARY ART HISTORY
description: SEM. 3. 6 hours of 3000-level art history courses. Or departmental approval. Examination of varying topics in modern and contemporary art history, 1750 CE-present. Course may be repeated for a maximum of 6 credit hours.

title: ADVANCED PHOTOGRAPHY
description: STU. 6. Pr. ARTS 3220 and ARTS 3230 . Advanced investigations of theory, history, and methods to inform photographic practice.  Emphasis on production of mature work and individual artistic identity. Frequent individual and group critiques. Course may be repeated for a maximum of 9 credit hours.

title: PAINTING IV
description: STU. 6. Pr. ARTS 3330 . Advanced painting with medium and subject idea determined by student with approval of the instructor. Emphasis on strengthening the student's awareness and technical skills as a maturing painter.  Nude models may be used. Course may be repeated for a maximum of 9 credit hours.

title: ADVANCED PRINTMAKING
description: STU. 6. Pr. ARTS 3410 and ARTS 3420 and ARTS 3430 . Individual research in printmaking.  Students focus on conceptual and technical development through continued research in relief, intaglio, or screen-printing. Course may be repeated for a maximum of 9 credit hours.

title: ADVANCED SCULPTURE
description: STU. 6. Pr. ARTS 3520 and ARTS 3530 . Advanced study of sculpture techniques and concepts. Students create an independently designed, instructor-approved body of work. Individual instruction and supervision of material exploration, research and reading. Course may be repeated for a maximum of 9 credit hours.

title: SENIOR CAPSTONE: ART HISTORY
description: SEM. 3. Declared ARTH major or minor and completion of 18 hours of 3000-level art history courses.  Capstone course for ARTH majors.

title: ADVANCED CERAMICS
description: STU. 6. Pr. ARTS 3820 and ARTS 3840 and ARTS 3850 . Advanced study of ceramic techniques and concepts. Student will create an independently-designed, instructor-approved body of work. Individual instruction and supervision of material exploration, research and reading. Frequent individual and group critiques. Course may be repeated for a maximum of 6 credit hours.

title: PROFESSIONAL STUDIO PRACTICES
description: LEC. 3. Pr. ARTS 3030 . Instruction in portfolio preparation, professional practices, and information on studio art careers and graduate study. Must have completed ARTS 3030 and nine credit hours in one concentration; taken concurrently with 4000-level studio in same concentration and prior to ARTS 4980 Senior Project in Studio Arts.

title: BFA CRITIQUE SEMINAR
description: LEC. 1.  SU. Pr. ARTS 3030 . Rigorous group critiques of artwork produced in media concentration courses, discussions and writing about art, art documentation, and exhibition practices. Course may be repeated for a maximum of 3 credit hours.

title: DIRECTED STUDIES
description: IND. Pr., Open only to ARTS students who have shown ability, initiative, and industry.  Departmental approval and 3.0 minimum GPA in 3000-level ARTS courses in area of directed study. Directed studies are offered in painting, printmaking, sculpture, art history, photography, animation, and ceramics. Course may be repeated for a maximum of 6 credit hours.

title: BA STUDIO ART CAPSTONE
description: LEC. 1.  SU. Students must have Senior Standing and have completed a minimum of 39 hours in the Major. Professional skills development and career strategies for BA Studio Art Majors.

title: HONORS SPECIAL PROBLEMS
description: LEC. Pr. Honors College. Course may be repeated for a maximum of 3 credit hours.

title: SPECIAL TOPICS IN STUDIO ART AND ART HISTORY
description: ST1/STU. 3. Topics in studio art and art history. Focus will vary according to the instructor. Departmental approval needed. Course may be repeated for a maximum of 6 credit hours.

title: SENIOR PROJECT FOR STUDIO ARTS
description: STU. 6. Pr. ARTS 3030 and ARTS 4860 and ( ARTS 3820 and ARTS 3840 and ARTS 3850 and ARTS 4840 ) or ( ARTS 3310 and ARTS 3320 and ARTS 3330 and ARTS 4840 ) or ( ARTS 3410 and ARTS 3420 and ARTS 3430 and ARTS 4440 ) or ( ARTS 3510 and ARTS 3520 and ARTS 3530 and ARTS 4540 ) or ( ARTS 3210 and ARTS 3220 and ARTS 3230 and ARTS 4240 ) or ( ARTS 3020 and ARTS 3120 and ARTS 3130 and ARTS 4140 ). And three additional courses in a single studio art concentration.  Must be taken in the student's final semester concurrent with third enrollment in ARTS 4860 . Directed terminal studio project with faculty-approved choice of content and medium.  Project will be exhibited.

title: HONORS RESEARCH AND THESIS
description: IND. Pr. Honors College. Course may be repeated for a maximum of 6 credit hours.

title: NUTRITIONAL BIOCHEMISTRY
description: LEC. 3. Pr. CHEM 2030 or CHEM 2070 or CHEM 2077 or CHEM 2080 or CHEM 2087 . Fundamental pathways of carbohydrate, lipid, and amino acid metabolism in human beings.

title: PRINCIPLES OF BIOCHEMISTRY
description: LEC. 3. Pr. ( BIOL 1010 or BIOL 1020 or BIOL 1030 or BIOL 1027 or BIOL 1037 ) and ( CHEM 2030 or CHEM 2070 or CHEM 2077 or CHEM 2080 or CHEM 2087 ). Structure and function of biomolecules, enzyme catalysis, processing of genetic information, bioenergetics and metabolism, and regulatory mechanisms in cellular processes.

title: BIOCHEMISTRY I
description: LEC. 3. Pr. CHEM 2080 or CHEM 2087 . Fundamentals of metabolism, focusing on the design and regulation of the major catabolic and biosynthetic metabolic pathways. Bioenergetics.

title: BIOCHEMISTRY I LABORATORY
description: LAB. 3. Pr. P/C BCHE 5180 or P/C CHEM 5180. Laboratory techniques required for identification and quantification of compounds of important biochemical classes.

title: BIOCHEMISTRY II
description: LEC. 3. Pr. BCHE 5180 or BCHE 6180 . Fundamentals of metabolism, focusing on the design and regulation of the major catabolic and biosynthetic metabolic pathways.

title: BIOCHEMISTRY II LABORATORY
description: LAB. 3. Pr. P/C BCHE 5190 or P/C CHEM 5190. Laboratory techniques required for partial purification, kinetic studies, and characterization of enzymes and nucleotides from various plants, animals and bacteria.

title: BIOCHEMISTRY I
description: LEC. 3. Pr. CHEM 2080 or CHEM 2087 . Departmental approval. Fundamentals of the classification, structure, and reactions of the major constituents of living matter and evaluation of binding phenomena and bioenergetics.

title: BIOCHEMISTRY I LABORATORY
description: LAB. 3. Pr. P/C BCHE 6180 or P/C CHEM 6180. Laboratory techniques required for identification and quantification of compounds of important biochemical classes.

title: BIOCHEMISTRY II
description: LEC. 3. Pr. BCHE 6180 . Departmental approval. Fundamentals of metabolism, focusing on the design and regulation of the major catabolic and biosynthetic metabolic pathways.

title: BIOCHEMISTRY II LABORATORY
description: LAB. 3. Pr. P/C CHEM 6190. Laboratory techniques required for partial purification, kinetic studies, and characterization of enzymes and nucleotides from various plants, animals, and bacteria.

title: ADVANCED BIOCHEMISTRY I
description: LEC. 3. Graduate credit will not be given for both BCHE 6190 and BCHE 7200 .

title: ADVANCED BIOCHEMISTRY II
description: LEC. 3. Structure and function of macromolecules participating in the flow of molecular information. Graduate credit will not be given for both BCHE 6180 and BCHE 7210 . Or equivalent.

title: PRINCIPLES OF CELLULAR AND MOLECULAR ENZYMOLOGY
description: LEC. 3. Pr. BCHE 6190 or CHEM 6190 or BCHE 7200 . Departmental approval. The principles of enzyme chemistry including the physical, chemical, and catalytic properties of enzymes.

title: BIOCHEMISTRY OF MACROMOLECULES
description: LEC. 3. Pr. BCHE 6180 or BCHE 7200 . Departmental approval. Advanced study of the structure of protein and nucleic acids: DNA replication, RNA transcription and protein synthesis.

title: BIOINFORMATICS
description: LEC. 3. Pr. BCHE 7210 . Departmental approval. Advanced study of main concepts and tools of genomics and proteomics.

title: BIOCHEMICAL RESEARCH TECHNIQUES
description: LEC. 3. Pr. BCHE 6190 . Modern biochemical laboratory techniques. Course may be repeated for a maximum of 6 credit hours.

title: TOPICS IN BIOCHEMISTRY
description: LEC. Pr. BCHE 7210 . Departmental approval. Directed studies in biochemistry. BCHE 7210 or equivalent. Course may be repeated for a maximum of 3 credit hours.

title: INTRODUCTION TO AVIATION
description: LEC. 3. Orientation to aviation management career opportunities. The history of significant events and accomplishments in the attempt to move through the air and space.

title: INTRODUCTION TO UNMANNED AIRCRAFT SYSTEMS (UAS)
description: (3) LEC. 3. Orientation to unmanned aircraft systems with emphasis on pilot and operating rules, National Airspace System (NAS) integration, safety, and commercial uses of Small UAS (sUAS).

title: THEORY OF FLIGHT
description: LEC. 2.5. Introduction to the many systems that make up the aviation ecosystem, including aircraft, the humans and organizations that interact with them, and the principles and aerodynamics of flight.

title: HUMAN FACTORS IN AVIATION
description: LEC. 3. Principles of human cognitive and physical performance, and man/machine interface and design, in aviation. Study of information processing, workload management, situational awareness, and decision-making.

title: LEADERSHIP FOR THE AVIATION PROFESSIONAL
description: LEC. 3. Pr. AVMG 1010 . AVMN and AVPF majors only. The course is designed to socialize the student into the roles, activities, and responsibilities essential for the emerging aviation leader, and is foundational to preparation for a career in the aviation industry or aviation-related environments.

title: AVIATION WEATHER
description: LEC. 3. Pr. AVMG 1010 . Meteorology as it applies to the operation of aircraft with emphasis on observation of weather elements and interpretation of flight planning weather information.

title: AIR TRANSPORT INDUSTRY DEVELOPMENT
description: LEC. 3. Pr. AVMG 1010 . Principles and analysis of air transport industry development, its regulatory environment, and associated certification processes.

title: APPLIED ANALYSIS IN AIR TRANSPORTATION
description: LEC. 3. Pr. ( AVMG 1010 or AVMG 1013) and ( ECON 2020 or ECON 2023 or ECON 2027 ). Economic theory and applications in the evolving air transport industry. Analysis of market forces and government regulation that drive resource allocation and industry performance.

title: AIRPORT OPERATIONS
description: LEC. 3. Pr. ( AVMG 1010 and AVMG 2400 ) or AVMF 2150 . Practices in managing airport operational systems at air carrier airports.

title: AIRCRAFT MAINTENANCE MANAGEMENT
description: LEC. 3. Pr. AVMG 1010 . Aircraft maintenance program fundamentals, procedures, and practices, with an emphasis on regulatory requirements.

title: STRESS MANAGEMENT IN AVIATION
description: LEC. 3. Pr. AVMG 2600 . Examine stress and stress management techniques related to pilot decision-making, situational awareness, crew coordination, communication, human error, fatigue, fitness, attitudes, workload management and Crew Resource Management (CRM). Senior, Junior, or departmental approval required.

title: PROFESSIONAL DEVELOPMENT IN AVIATION
description: LEC. 1. AVMN and AVPF majors only. Career planning and preparation for aviation internships and professional experience opportunities.

title: BUSINESS AVIATION MANAGEMENT
description: LEC. 3. Pr. AVMG 1010 . Current principles and practices in commercial and business/corporate flight operations including organizational sources of revenue, functions, domestic and international operations, maintenance requirements, and the importance of a sound safety culture.

title: COMMERCIAL AVIATION SAFETY
description: LEC. 3. Pr. AVMG 1010 . Analysis of safety principles and regulatory practices of the FAA, NTSB, and ICAO, emphasizing FAA and ICAO Safety Management Systems.

title: AIR TRANSPORT PLANNING
description: LEC. 3. Pr. AVMG 3140 and AVMG 3200 and AVMG 3600 . Management decision making involved in selection of equipment, routes and the establishment of rates by certified and non-certified air carriers.

title: AIRPORT MANAGEMENT
description: LEC. 3. Pr. AVMG 3050 . Practices in management of a civil public airport, including organization, functions, operations, sources of revenue, funding, maintenance and administration.

title: AIRPORT PLANNING AND DESIGN
description: LEC. 3. Pr. AVMG 4130 . Principles and procedures pertaining to planning airport facilities required to meet the immediate and future air transportation of a community or region.

title: AIRSPACE MANAGEMENT
description: LEC. 3. Pr. AVMG 3050 . Air traffic control procedures, facilities, center, and operations. Theory of radar operation and air traffic separation.

title: AIR CARGO OPERATIONS
description: LEC. 3. Pr. AVMG 1010 . Domestic and international air cargo operations with emphasis on cargo economics, equipment, domestic and international regulatory activities, agents, operational techniques, systems and problems.

title: INTERNSHIP IN AVIATION MANAGEMENT
description: INT. Practical on-the-job training under supervision with aviation agencies. Written reports are required by designated faculty supervisors. Course may be repeated for a maximum of 6 credit hours.

title: HONORS SPECIAL PROBLEMS
description: LEC. 1-3. Pr. Honors College. Honors College. Special topics presented to Honors College students. Course may be repeated for a maximum of 3 credit hours.

title: HONORS THESIS
description: IND. Pr. Honors College. Honors College. Thesis for Honors College students. Course may be repeated for a maximum of 3 credit hours.

title: AVIATION LAW AND POLICY
description: LEC. 3. Pr. AVMG 1010 . The legal structure of aviation including federal, local and state statutes, contracts, insurance and liability, regulatory statutes, and case law.

title: AIRLINE MANAGEMENT
description: LEC. 3. Pr. AVMG 1010 and AVMG 3050 . Airline manufacturing, economic, and operational/managerial issues, research and development and competition issues and a survey of the world's major airlines in terms of their management strategies and style.

title: GLOBAL AIR TRANSPORTATION MANAGEMENT
description: LEC. 3. Pr. AVMG 3140 and AVMG 3200 . Junior Standing or departmental approval. The economic development of international air transportation from its beginnings to present day. Explores a wide range of international aviation issues such as bilateral and open skies agreements, airline mega alliances, and joint ventures.

title: SPECIAL TOPICS IN AVIATION MANAGEMENT
description: LEC. 3. Investigation of current issues in the aviation industry.

title: AVIATION LAW AND POLICY
description: LEC. 3. The legal structure of aviation including federal, local, and state statutes, contracts, insurance and liability, regulatory statutes and case law.

title: AIRLINE MANAGEMENT
description: LEC. 3. Departmental approval. Airline manufacturing, economic, and operation/managerial issues, research and development and competition issues and a survey of the world's major airlines in terms of their management strategies and style.

title: GLOBAL AIR TRANSPORTATION MANAGEMENT
description: LEC. 3. Pr. AVMG 3140 and AVMG 3200 or departmental approval. International foreign air carriers, influences of ICAO and IATA, national ownership, determinants of power, operational and management practices, routes and fares/ Junior standing

title: SPECIAL TOPICS AVIATION MNGT
description: LEC. 1-3. Departmental approval. Investigation of current issues in the aviation industry. Credit will not be given for both AVMG 5970 and AVMG 6970 . Course may be repeated for a maximum of 9 credit hours.

title: SPECIAL PROBLEMS AVIATION MNGT
description: LEC. 1-3. Departmental approval. Special problems and current status of the aviation and aerospace industries are analyzed though a problem solving exercise. Course may be repeated for a maximum of 6 credit hours.

title: INTRODUCTION TO TECHNOLOGY DESIGN
description: LEC. 2. LAB. 3. Introduction to the design process, 2D and 3D parametric solid modeling, and both manual and automated fabrication processes.

title: DIGITAL ANALYTICS IN AGRICULTURE AND TECHNOLOGY
description: LEC. 2. LAB. 1. Pr. BATM 1110 or BIOP 2120 . An introduction to creative and analytical methods to solve technological problems. Define the problem, explore strategies, select and implement solutions, and evaluate results.

title: COMPUTER AIDED DESIGN TECHNOLOGY
description: LEC. 2. LAB. 1. Introductory course in computer aided design (CAD) and land mapping. Students gain competence in CAD operations used to fabricate parts and to develop field- and watershed-scale maps. Class and project topics include drawing for mechanical part fabrication and scale mapping for construction site development and agricultural field management. Must be in Junior standing Course may be repeated for a maximum of 6 credit hours.

title: AG TECHNOLOGY GEOSPATIAL APPLICATIONS
description: LEC. 2. LAB. 3. Pr. BATM 1110 and ( PHYS 1500 or PHYS 1600 ). Geospatial applications for agricultural land resource management. Course introduces equipment and practices used in conventional land surveying and geospatial mapping as they interface with global positioning systems (GPS), geographic information systems (GIS), and computer-aided design (CAD).

title: NATURAL RESOURCE SYSTEMS CONSERVATION
description: LLB. Pr. ( MATH 1130 or MATH 1133 or MATH 1150 or MATH 1610 or MATH 1680 ) and ( PHYS 1500 or PHYS 1600 ). Natural resource conservation technologies including rainfall-runoff relationships, sediment transport capacity, runoff control structures, water supply development, surveying techniques including GPS methods.

title: AGRICULTURAL POWER AND MACHINERY FUNDAMENTALS
description: LLB. Pr. ( MATH 1130 or MATH 1133 or MATH 1150 or MATH 1610 or MATH 1680 ) and ( PHYS 1500 or PHYS 1600 ). Power unit fundamentals with emphasis on diesel and small gasoline engines; mechanics of operation, safety, use, and adjustment of machines used for horticultural and agronomic crop production; and precision agriculturr principles and technology.

title: AGRICULTURAL PRODUCTION AND PROCESSING FACILITY TECHNOLOGY
description: LEC. 3. Pr. MATH 1130 or MATH 1133 or MATH 1150 or MATH 1610 or MATH 1680 . Fundamental requirements for the design and operation of agricultural production and processing facilities.

title: TURF SYSTEMS IRRIGATION DESIGN
description: LEC. 3. Pr. MATH 1120 or MATH 1130 or MATH 1133 or MATH 1150 or MATH 1610 or MATH 1680 . Irrigation system design for turf-based systems including residential lawns, commercial properties, athletic fields, and golf courses. Irrigation scheduling and water demand are presented to provide management capabilities.

title: PROFESSIONAL PRACTICE IN TECHNOLOGY MANAGEMENT
description: LEC. 1. LAB. 3. Pr. BATM 5110 . First in the two-course capstone experience. This course focuses on professional topics that prepare students for technical careers; teamwork, communication, standards and codes, economics, project and time management. Teams initiate the capstone design project.

title: TECHNOLOGY CAPSTONE
description: LEC. 1. LAB. 6. Pr. BATM 4100 . Development and evaluation of a team-based capstone project using tools from the technology curriculum; emphasizing communication, critical thinking, and technical and economic analyses.

title: SPECIAL PROBLEMS IN AGRICULTURAL TECHNOLOGY MANAGEMENT
description: IND. Departmental approval. Faculty supervision of individual student investigations of specialized problems in biological and agricultural technology management. May be repeated with change in problem. Course may be repeated for a maximum of 6 credit hours.

title: SPECIAL TOPICS IN AGRICULTURAL TECHNOLOGY MANAGEMENT
description: LLB. Departmental approval. Investigation of current issues in biological and agricultural technology management. Course may be repeated for a maximum of 6 credit hours.

title: UNDERGRADUATE RESEARCH
description: LLB. Departmental approval. Directed research in the area of specialty within the department. Course may be repeated for a maximum of 6 credit hours.

title: AGRI-INDUSTRIAL ELECTRICAL APPLICATIONS
description: LEC. 2. LAB. 3. Pr. BATM 2110 and ( PHYS 1500 or PHYS 1600 ). An introduction to the fundamentals of electricity and electrical systems used in agricultural and industrial applications. Electricity basics include safety, AC (single and three phase) and DC power. Selecting and sizing components include wiring conductors, safety devices, motors, other loads.

title: AGRI-INDUSTRIAL ELECTRONICS AND CONTROLS
description: LEC. 2. LAB. 3. Pr. BATM 5110 . An introduction to the fundamentals of electronic control systems used in agricultural and industrial production and processing applications. Electronic control system components include programmable logic controllers (PLCs), switches, relays, sensors, and ladder logic.

title: PRECISION AG TECHNOLOGY
description: LEC. 2. LAB. 3. Pr. BATM 3510 . An overview of the principles of precision agriculture with focus on prescriptive agriculture and the ability to effectively execute input management plans using today's technologies. Course material and discussions will include how technologies such as GPS, agricultural GIS, sensors for qualitative and quantitative measurement of soil and plant variables, variable-rate technology are being implement with data informing sub-field level management and subsequent farm business decisions.

title: COMMERCIAL POULTRY & LIVESTOCK HOUSING
description: LEC. 2. LAB. 3. Pr. STAT 2510 . An introduction to the basic design, operation, and maintenance of modern commercial animal housing systems. Emphasis will be placed on poultry and swine systems with elements of dairy and beef when applicable.

title: DRONE TECHNOLOGIES IN AGRICULTURAL AND BIOLOGICAL SYSTEMS
description: LEC. 2. LAB. 3. Pr. STAT 2510 and PHYS 1500 . Drone or Unmanned Aerial Vehicles (UAV) technologies and the data generated from these systems are being utilized in agricultural, biological and natural resource systems. Topics will include UAV types, sensors and application systems for drones, sensor resolution and calibration, flight and mission planning techniques. Image and date collection techniques, analysis, interpretation and decision making, generation of prescriptive maps, and other emerging drone technologies.

title: PRINCIPLES OF FOOD ENGINEERING TECHNOLOGY
description: LEC. 3. LAB. 3. Pr. ( MATH 1130 or MATH 1133 or MATH 1150 or MATH 1153 or MATH 1610 or MATH 1613 or MATH 1617 ) and ( PHYS 1000 or PHYS 1003 or PHYS 1007 or PHYS 1500 or PHYS 1600 or PHYS 1607 ). Engineering concepts and unit operations used in processing food products. Fall.
